title: Our First Spring on the Farm
excerpt: The frost finally broke and the pasture is greening up. Here's what our first real spring on Webb's Wild Acres has looked like so far.
content:
There's something almost holy about that first warm morning when you step out onto the porch and realize the air smells different. The frost has finally broken. The grass that was gray and brittle is showing the faintest tint of green at the roots, and the old oak along the fence line has little buds pinched along every branch.

We moved out to our ten acres in the Arkansas hills last autumn, and all winter we were living on faith — faith that the land would wake back up, that the pasture would recover, that the old soil under our feet was still rich enough to matter. This spring is answering all of that.

Our two ewes, Clover and Biscuit, have been the best barometers. When they started pacing the fence line and pulling at the grass near the gate, we knew it was time to rotate them into the south pasture. Watching them move through that fresh growth, heads down, utterly content, was one of the most satisfying things I've seen since we got here.

We've also been working the kitchen garden beds. Last fall we sheet-mulched two 4x8 beds with cardboard and wood chips, and now that we've pulled it back, the soil underneath is dark and crumbly — full of earthworms doing their slow, faithful work. We planted garlic in October on a hunch, and the green shoots are already six inches tall.

There's so much more to do than there are hours in the day. The fence along the north field needs three new posts. The chicken coop roof lost a piece of tin in a February ice storm. The herb beds need turning. But standing in the middle of it all, with the meadowlarks calling and the dogs flopped in a patch of sun, it doesn't feel overwhelming. It feels like exactly where we're supposed to be.

title: Why We Chose Arkansas
excerpt: We looked at land in three states before we found our ten acres in the Ozark foothills. Here's what brought us here — and why we haven't looked back.
content:
People always want to know: why Arkansas? We get that question from friends in the city, from family who thought we were moving to Vermont, from neighbors back home who couldn't quite picture it on a map.

The honest answer is that we followed the land prices, the rainfall maps, and our gut — in roughly that order.

We spent nearly two years researching homestead land in the South and the Midwest. We looked seriously at the Tennessee hill country, a few parcels in southern Missouri, and a beautiful but expensive piece in the North Carolina mountains. Every time we ran the numbers, Arkansas kept coming back into focus. The Ozark foothills get real rain — 45 to 50 inches a year. The growing season is long, 180 days or better. Land is still priced like it's 1995 compared to other regions, which meant we could actually afford acreage with a creek, a woodlot, and room to build.

But numbers only take you so far. The real answer is that we drove down one March weekend to look at a few parcels, and something about the quality of the light in the late afternoon — the way it came sideways through the cedar trees and made the whole hillside glow amber — just got us. My wife stood at the edge of the south pasture on our place and said, quietly, 'This is it.' And it was.

The community here has been another revelation. Our nearest neighbors raise heritage hogs and have forgotten more about growing sweet corn than most people will ever know. The local farm co-op has been invaluable. The farmers markets are small but serious — people selling real food they actually grew.

Arkansas doesn't get the romantic treatment that Vermont or the Pacific Northwest gets. It doesn't have a brand. But for our kind of farming — small-scale, diversified, close to the land — it might just be the best-kept secret in America.
